patient.medical_history: previous shoulder strain from playing basketball

timeline.summary: Triaged as SEMI_URGENT. Patient has mild shoulder discomfort with intermittent stiffness. Sent to doctor for further evaluation.

details.notes: Vital signs are stable; symptoms indicate a non-life-threatening concern.

timeline.summary: Ordering Shoulder MRI due to the need for assessment of potential underlying issues such as tears or impingement. Found 3 similar cases suggesting this approach. Will diagnose after results.

details.context_for_next: Assessment of shoulder discomfort and need for MRI.
details.reasoning: Patient presents with mild shoulder discomfort, similar cases indicate potential partial thickness tears or shoulder impingement syndrome. No tests have been completed yet. Ordering MRI for better assessment of the shoulder tissues and to rule out any significant tears or other pathologies.

timeline.summary: MRI completed. Results will be shared with your doctor.

details.test_outcome: Finding: Partial tear of the supraspinatus tendon, mild fluid accumulation in the subacromial bursa, no significant bone abnormalities noted.
details.viewer_output: MRI completed. Results will be shared with your doctor.

timeline.summary: Diagnosis: Partial tear of the supraspinatus tendon. Found 3 similar cases with successful outcomes. Treatment: Physical therapy focused on strengthening and retraining shoulder muscles.

details.diagnosis: Partial tear of the supraspinatus tendon
details.recommended_treatment: Physical therapy focused on strengthening and retraining shoulder muscles, potentially a corticosteroid injection if pain persists.
details.context_for_next: Shoulder discomfort diagnosed and treatment plan outlined.

final_diagnosis.viewer_output: Diagnosis: Partial tear of the supraspinatus tendon. Found 3 similar cases with successful outcomes. Treatment: Physical therapy focused on strengthening and retraining shoulder muscles.

validation_reason: LLM (high): A partial tear of the supraspinatus tendon is a specific type of rotator cuff tear, as the supraspinatus is one of the four rotator cuff muscles. Therefore, both terms refer to the same underlying condition, with the former being more specific.
